title: Anne Wojcicki at TEDMED 2009
text:
[Music]
when I first met Linda ay my co-founder
we both had this amazing frustration we
both had this realization that the
consumer is really underutilized in
healthcare so if I'm an academic you
know I'm a physician I I own my data um
I go to my physician I ask them
questions if I'm the Pharma company I'm
trying to develop the drugs but that the
consumer really wasn't that well um
utilized and we wanted to create a
platform for for individuals to have a
voice because we didn't think that we
could actually make change happen from
within the system we said we need to
actually create something that's totally
outside of the system that's going to
give all of us power to make a
difference and and that power to make a
difference is the ability to say I want
my genome and I want to be able to
network with other people about their
genomes or about specific diseases
that's 23 and me so I have my first case
study this is my poor unfortunate
roommate from college Monica Monica who
you know worked in film she knew nothing
about biology I made her spit when we
started the company she was like I
really could care less about my DNA um
but you know Monica had some questions
you know she knows she knows obviously
both her parents she knows some about
her background but she didn't know a lot
but you know if you look at her like
what is she is she Jewish
African-American Hispanic Native
American can you tell is she at risk for
ma generation sickle cell is Plavix
going to work in her can she drink three
cups of coffee a day all kinds of
question about her health so just as a
little background 23 me has a service
where we can actually tell you some
information about your ancestry now this
is sort of going to be a a good
percentage of the people in this room
this is sort of the typical European
background so 100% European this is one
of The Offspring of 23 and me one parent
is Asian and one parent is European so
you can see it's sort of half and half
now this is
Monica so Monica is quite colorful and
it was this I showed this this slide
once and people did come up after the
talk and say is she single um the
question here is is how do you actually
how you know if she went to the doctor's
office what would you say like what
would she fill out on the form you know
if she was wanted to be tested for for
braa and they asked you know are you of
Jewish descent they probably would have
no idea so if I look at that
specifically at braa 1 braa 2 well what
is that you know it's sort of part on
the European part part on the Asian part
part on the African part like I don't
know it's mostly found in the in the
ashkanazi population but so what is it
so it really emphasized the point to us
that you can't necessarily tell that
much much just by looking at someone so
Monica also became an interesting story
because again as I mentioned she wasn't
that interested in her DNA until she saw
the ancestry and was like wow that was
pretty cool and then unfortunately after
a long flight back from London she had a
pain in her leg and lo and behold it
turned out she had a blood clot and
Monica um having her DNA was like wow
you know I actually have my warrin
report so again lo and behold Monica has
increased Warren sensitivity so she may
require a lower dose so she actually
took that to the doctor and said you
know warrin is typically a drug that
it's life savings but it's difficult to
dose you get too much of it you can
actually get you can bleed to death you
get too little of it it doesn't doesn't
successfully break up the clot so this
was suddenly really in useful
information for Monica but the way the
current health care system works it's
not you're never going to get that
information ahead of time you know for
again this goes back to the mission of
the company for me it's it was worth it
for me if I paid $400 I want that
information ahead of time but for the
insurance companies it says well it's
it's easier just to try dosing you you
know give you a little bit too much you
bleed we'll adjust it I don't really
feel like bleeding or I don't feel like
you know not successfully having my
blood clot dismantled so so it BEC was
really helpful information for
her this is sort of a snapshot of the
offering that we're having now we have
four drug responses pharmacogenetic
reports now Warren abacavir Plavix and
five Fu so Plavix is actually a really
interesting example because it's the
world's it's one of the world's
bestselling drugs if not the the world's
bestselling drug now and in 30% of
patients it doesn't work so Plavix is
used for acute coronary syndrome and
other things if you get a stent you
might get pavic afterwards but but would
that suck if you start taking it and
it's not really working and and then you
have another acute coronary syndrome so
why don't why don't we test ahead of
time so again what the response that I
usually get from the current system is
well it's cheaper just to see if you if
you're not responding then we just
switch your your therapy so it becomes a
question like I can't fault the system
because that's just the way the system
works but as an individual I want that
opportunity now to actually take more
control over my health and that's
fundamentally what we think is that
there should be more and more of these
opportunities and that again is the
mission of of the company have that
opportunity to take
charge one of the areas that that
probably gets more U more of the cont is
the risk prediction if I could tell you
that you're at higher risk for breast
cancer or prostate cancer what would you
do and and granted it's a it's a great
debate some people want to know and some
people don't want to know you don't want
to know you don't have to know but but
we're never going to get to that that
era of preventative care if I can't find
out ahead of time what do you know what
am I actually at risk for so I can look
at family history there's a lot of
people who don't know their family
history you know I had you my family
came over from Eastern Europe I only
know one or two generations back I have
no idea what people died of and people
didn't talk about their diseases in the
past so I want to know what am I at
increased risk for and this was actually
a very personal example for us where my
husband's family has a lot of
Parkinson's in it and I wanted to know
is he at increased risk so I knew that
there was actually a marker out there
that's commonly found in ashkanazi
Jewish populations and I wanted him to
get him test it I couldn't get anyone to
test him for it they all said it's so
unlikely that he has it and even if he
had it what would you do you wouldn't do
anything um sort of all the typical
arguments so the great thing about
having your company you can sort of
encourage people to do something so our
our science team conveniently put this
on our chip the ability to test for this
m this variation for Parkinson's and lo
and behold it turns out my husband's
family has it and my husband you can see
here you know averaged about 1.6 people
out of a 100 are likely for developing
Parkinson's wow he's over
50% my God we need to do something
and so this has actually been a huge
kick in the butt you know I've always
told a may you know you're getting a
little fat um but but now he suddenly
like now he's suddenly quite fit um he
works out every day he rides his bike he
actually watches what he eats he's
really worried about it and I think a
lot of it a lot of it came from just
this you know we're really motivated to
say if he's at increased risk for
Parkinson and 58 you know over 50%
chance well my God there's also there's
40 of those people who are not going to
get Parkinson's what what is it that we
can do that's going to enable him to
prevent getting Parkinson's and that
also becomes one of the main missions
for 203 and me so this gets into the
research 2.0 concept where what we're
trying to do is you get access to your
genetic information and then you have
the ability to fill out a survey and 23
and me has a whole science team that's
now trying to say we get all your
genetics we get all the results from
your survey information now we're going
to start trying to understand what does
this genetic information mean because we
know some of what genetic information
means but we we've just barely scratched
the surface I feel like it's aggressive
even saying that we scratch the surface
because we know so little of this
information so we have a lot more to
find and for instance in my husband's
example if I know he's at high risk I
really want to know what is it that we
can do that's going to prevent it and I
feel confident that there's going to be
some things out there it might be that
there's some drug therapy that comes
along it might be that there's certain
kind of vitamins it might be a really
vigorous kind of exercise there's
something out there that we can that we
can discover that's going to be able to
help and prevent it so 23 me is really
focused on the research we've been
really secretive about how big our
database size actually is but it's at
that point where we're actually starting
to publish and we're making discoveries
so we're happy to say that the database
now is over 30,000 genomes of I should
say active genomes of people who are
actually participating on the site and
over 70% of our users are filling out at
least one survey so you can imagine we
have a tremendous amount of data
that's coming out our science team is
just in in heaven if any of you want to
research data just let me know so what
we're also really proud of in the last
two years we've successfully been able
to create one of the world's largest
databases of genotype phenotype
information so I asked our science team
to try to tell me which databases were
larger and and they didn't want to be
too arrogant of saying oh we're the
largest already I mean there's decode
and there's some of the other groups
that have it but we have an amazing
asset here and what we're hoping to do
is to figure out again from the personal
mission of all of us we want better
healthare and we want it now and I want
certain things like if there's a drug
response if I can find out if I'm not
going to respond to a drug I want to
know that now so we're going to we're
going to work with all the different
disease advocacy groups and other things
try to figure out how do we empower the
individuals to come and again try to
make Healthcare better for all of
us so this is an example of some of the
surveys we have we have some fun things
um I'll just do a quick survey how many
people actually smell the asparagus when
they eat it in their
pee and how many people have no idea
what I'm talking about are you too
embarrass oh it's always it's always
very interesting we had a we had an
example once with a camera crew where
they just stared us like what are you
talking about um it's always fun when
people don't know um so we have we have
over 20 surveys now um asking from all
kinds of trivial things like you know
the asparagus question to you know when
you clasp your hands which thumb is on
top um to the more serious where we have
actually you know um 10 you know
Healthcare traits about you what kind of
um over the drug uh over the counter
medications so things like benad dril
how many of you actually get hyper when
you take benad
dril yeah you just want to know that
probably if you have your children and
they accidentally take it um so all
kinds of research topics that we're
continuing to pursue and this is this
has been growing pretty rapidly I'm also
happy to announce this was an abstract
that just came out at the American
Society of human genetics last week we
had Publications I should say pending
but you can see that this method is
yielding results where we are actually
finding novel associations in fact we
have results on 22 common traits that
are going to be in this uh
publication and if you want to look at
the ashg website you can probably find
it so we also have two major research
initiatives underway one with the
Michael J fox Foundation where we were
um enrolled over 3,000 Parkinson's
patients within a month from start to
finish we started that project uh in
January December of this year at the
faster cures conference we are going to
be announcing data from that um so
anyone who knows things about clinical
trials will realize this is a phenomenal
timeline how quickly we've been able to
enroll people study them and actually
have some kind of data healthy aging is
a project that we just did with the
senior games um the senior games were in
palota this year and we thought it was a
phenomenal cohort people between uh
really over 55 and and 80 um
participating in in a multitude of
athletic events we offered free
genotyping to them we have over 5,000
people now filling out surveys um and
for us this is phenomenal it's a healthy
older cohort um so again that's an
that's a population that is difficult to
usually find um as part of studies we
just really love this graph this is
actually a visual representation of our
database a subset of our database and
how people are sharing so one of the
unique things about 23 and me again is
that we want individuals to be able to
communicate with each other so we find
there's a very active
number of active uh dialogues on
Parkinson's and all the other diseases
and individuals find and they connect to
each other and then they start to share
and you can see this guy is obviously a
super sharer he's he has a lot of
friends and all the other little blue
dots are the nodes but you can see that
sharing is incredibly popular on the
site so one of the other things that has
come out of having a large database now
is that people started to realize that
they could start to find lost family
members we' created a product where you
could compare yourself to your siblings
or your your your distant cousins and
actually see where you share DNA in
common and then all of a sudden all
these people started saying I I have
random pieces of DNA in common with you
know my friend down the street so we
actually did um a little experimenting
and and um we're also happy to announce
that we are introducing a new feature
that's going to launch in the next
couple weeks called relative finder the
majority of ancestry products test
really either the maternal lineage or
the paternal lineage so if you see on
this tree that's really just one branch
of the whole tree the mitochondrial DNA
is your mother's mother's mother why
chromosome is father's father's father
so it's a limit what we can do now is we
can fill out the whole tree and so all
those cousins that you may or may not
know um we can actually now start to
identify this is a quote that actually
just popped up we just launched this in
beta for the last couple weeks but as
you can see people are finding lost
relatives and it's phenomenal for
populations who have had to move over
the last century and don't have for
instance you know my grandmother had
eight siblings and we don't know we know
we know a couple of them but we don't
know where the most of the other
siblings their descendants went so now
suddenly you're actually starting to be
able to connect these
people so this is sort of an example
what my account looks like um you can
see there I have my my parent and child
um you can see siblings uncles nephews
so it's actually identifying I've
obviously made a number of my family
members spit um but but look I have I
have a second cousin that I'm not
connected with I have a feeling I I
think I know who it is um but but it's
pretty interesting and then look at all
my third cousins fourth cousins fifth
cousins but I have over I have almost
over 900 cousins in this database so
granted a lot of them are pretty distant
but I have a lot of near term or close
relatives between you know before
between first second third fourth
cousins so it's become phenomenally
interesting to start to look at this
thank
[Music]
you
